Page 3
Helen Vlassara, MD Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai	Helen Vlassara, MD Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai
Title photo: iStock.com/
have flavo
What if you have a spare
	$1,000? Perhaps the money
	is a tax refund…a lottery
	win…an inheritance…or
	some other windfall.
	How about not spending the money
	but investing it instead? Don’t let the
	fact that it’s “just” $1,000 stop you.
	There are a number of especially smart
	things to do with a thousand (or a few
	thousand) bucks whether you want to
	save up for a special purchase…add to
	your nest egg…or even invest the money
	as a gift for your kids or grandchildren.
	To help our readers choose what to do
	with their next thousand, Bottom Line
	Personal spoke with five investment
	experts. The right investment choice
	for you will depend, in part, on when
	you might need to draw on the money
	and what your risk tolerance is…	
GET 3.5% ON A SAVINGS BOND	
Surprisingly, you (or your gift recipi-
	ent) can double the $1,000 in 20 years
	with a US savings bond. That’s because
	Series EE bonds will pay a guaranteed
	3.5% annual return. The catch is that
	the money must stay in the savings
	bond for 20 years to be a good invest-
	ment—if it is redeemed early, it will
	pay only 0.1% annually. But that 3.5%
is much better than what you can get
	on a 20-year US Treasury Bond, which
	currently yields about 2.2%. You can
	buy and sell EE bonds through an on-
	line account at TreasuryDirect.gov. You
	generally can’t redeem in the first year,
	and if you redeem in the first five years,
	you forfeit three months’ interest.	
Brian J. Kurtz is an estate-
	planning specialist with Assured
	Investment Planners, Troy, Michi-
	gan, and author of Getting the Most
	Out of Your Savings Bonds.
GET 2% ON A CD	
Yields on CDs are so low right now
	that you need to choose one with a
HEARD BY OUR EDITORS	
Weight gained from Thanksgiving
	until around New Year’s takes five
	months to lose, warns Brian Wansink,
	PhD. During the holiday season: Except
	for salad and vegetables, keep all other
	serving dishes off the table when eating.
	Place them on a counter. That way you
	have to leave the table to get seconds—
	and so are less likely to do so. To limit
	the temptation of leftovers: Keep them
	out of sight by freezing them or wrap-
	ping them in aluminum foil.
	Brian Wansink, PhD, is director of Cornell Food
	and Brand Lab, Ithaca, New York, and author of	
Slim by Design and coauthor of a study of holiday
	weight gain, published in The New England Jour-
	nal of Medicine.
Out-of-network ATM fees have
	climbed to a record $4.57 per transac-
	tion, on average (that’s $237.64 a year
	if you use an ATM once a week), says
	personal finance expert Greg McBride,
	CFA. This includes two separate fees—
	one charged by the financial institu-
	tion that provides the ATM (averaging
	$2.90) plus a second one by the ATM
	user’s own financial
	institution (averag-
	ing $1.67). To avoid
	out-of-network ATM
	fees: Check whether
	your bank offers a
	free smartphone app
	that can direct you to the closest fee-
	free ATM. Or use your debit card to get
	cash when you make a purchase at a
	retailer that offers fee-free cash back.
	Greg McBride, CFA, is chief financial analyst at
	personal-finance website Bankrate.com, which
	does an annual survey of ATM fees.	
Vasectomy does not increase
	prostate cancer risk as commonly
	believed, reports Sheldon Marks, MD.
	A new, very thorough study by epide-
	miologists from the American Cancer
	Society found no link between vasec-
	tomy—an effective and inexpensive
	form of long-term birth control—and
	prostate cancer.
	Sheldon Marks, MD, is a urologist and micro-
	surgical specialist in private practice in Tucson,
	Arizona. The study of vasectomy and prostate
	cancer reviewed data on almost 364,000 men
	and was published in Journal of Clinical Oncology.
> >

The Best Ways to Invest
	$1,000 Now	
The Best Ways to Invest
	$1,000 Now	
The Best Ways to Invest
	$1,000 Now
Title photo: iStock.com/
Bottom Line Personal interviewed Susan David,
	PhD, psychologist on the faculty of Harvard
	Medical School, Boston, and cofounder and co-
	director of the Institute of Coaching at McLean
	Hospital in Belmont, Massachusetts. She is
	author of Emotional Agility: Get
	Unstuck, Embrace Change and
	Thrive in Work and Life. If you
	want to assess how effective you
	are with your moods and emo-
	tions, a free quiz is offered at
	SusanDavid.com/learn.
have flav
Many people embrace hap-
	piness but try to push
	aside sadness and anger.
	Our culture encourages
	this—books about how to be happy
	frequently find their way onto best-
	seller lists, and “the pursuit of happi-
	ness” is written into our Declaration
	of Independence. Sadness and anger
	are viewed as problems to overcome
	on the road to happiness.
	But these so-called “negative” emo-
	tions can be useful. Here are some of
	the powerful benefits of bad moods and
	how to use them to your advantage…	
n Bad moods make us more careful
	and less gullible. When people are hap-
	py, they tend to have an “everything will
	be all right” mentality—which can get
	them into trouble when everything is
	not all right. When people are unhappy,
	on the other hand, their minds are on a
higher state of alert, carefully analyzing
	what they see and hear. That’s a useful
	mind-set to have when digging through
	potentially important details or dealing
	with slick salespeople.	
What to do: If you soon will need
	to be detail-oriented or skeptical—for
	example, if you’re on your way to nego-
	tiate a major purchase—you can inten-
	tionally put yourself in a negative mood.
	Recall an unhappy but not devastating
	memory, such as a time when your career
	hit a rough patch. Or listen to a piece of
	music that always makes you feel mel-
	ancholy—Samuel Barber’s “Adagio for
	Strings” may be a good choice.	
n Bad moods make us more con-
	vincing. People who are in negative
HEARD BY OUR EDITORS	
Avoid CDs with returns linked to
	stock market performance, says
	Keith Amburgey, CFA, CFP. Sold through
	banks and brokerages, market-linked	
CDs do not pay fixed annual yields…
	they cap their market-based gains, typi-
	cally at 5% per month…and monthly
	losses usually are capped but at a high
	30%. Market-linked CDs charge a com-
	mission as high as 3% on your initial
	investment, although they guarantee
	that you will end up with at least as
	much money as you invested. A recent
	study found that since 2010, nearly two-
	thirds of market-linked CDs underper-
	formed traditional five-year CDs.
	Keith Amburgey, CFA, CFP, is CEO of Rutherford
	Asset Planning, Tampa. RutherfordAsset.com	
Prostate cancer treatments com-
	pared in a new study, reports Jenny
	Donovan, PhD. Prostate cancer pa-
	tients with malignancies that haven’t
	spread and who are monitored but
	don’t receive treatment have a nearly
	identical risk for death over the next
	decade as those who choose surgery
	and/or radiation. Surgery and/or radi-
	ation (with possible side effects such
	as incontinence and/or loss of sexual
	function) lowered the risk of the cancer
	spreading but did not result in lower
	risk for death over 10 years. Note: Men
	with advanced or aggressive prostate
	cancer may need prompt treatment.
	Jenny Donovan, PhD, professor of social medi-
	cine, University of Bristol, UK.	
Best way to keep track of paper
	US savings bonds—convert them to
	electronic form, we hear from financial
	planner Jonathan D. Pond. This makes
	it easier to check
	a bond’s value
	and to redeem
	it. To learn how
	to switch, go to
	TreasuryDirect.
	gov, put “convert paper bonds” in the
	search box, and look on the results
	page for “Convert Your Paper Savings
	Bonds Using SmartExchange.”
	Jonathan D. Pond is president of Jonathan
	D. Pond, LLC, an investment advisory firm in
	Newton, Massachusetts. JonathanPond.com
> >

Why Bad
	Moods Are
	Good	
Susan David, PhD	
Harvard Medical School
VOLUME 37
NUMBER 21
NOVEMBER 1, 2016 / $5
36YEARS / INSIDE INFORMATION FROM THE WORLD’S BEST EXPERTS	
Do-good fund
	beats market	
Page 3
Title photo: iStock.com/
Bottom Line Personal interviewed Kenneth
	G. Winans, president and founder of Winans
	Investments, an investment advisory firm in
	Novato, California, and a former
	investment adviser at Merrill
	Lynch. He is coauthor of three
	books on investing, including	
Investment Atlas. He also writes
	a column for Forbes. Winans
	Investments.com
have flavo
The tumultuous world is a scary
	place for investors nowadays.
	Yet many advisers still tell
	American investors to keep
	their portfolios diversified by holding
	foreign stocks. The idea is that hold-
	ing a mixture of US and foreign stocks
	will reduce volatility and maybe even
	increase returns.
But is that really true? Is it good
	advice?
Kenneth G. Winans says that for
	Americans to think they have to hold
	foreign stocks is a big mistake. In fact,
	the veteran investment manager tells
	his clients that they don’t need to look
	outside the US at all. He says that’s
	especially true right now, when Europe
	is in an economic rut…China is bat-
	tling a sharp slowdown in growth…
	Japan has been unable to kick-start its
	stagnant economy…and low oil prices
	have pummeled countries ranging from
	Venezuela and Canada to Saudi Arabia
and Russia. And Winans believes the
	all-American approach makes sense for
	the long term, too.
	We asked him why he holds that be-
	lief and why investors should consider
	adopting this approach…	
GLOBAL DANGERS	
Foreign stocks have long been subpar
	performers and a poor hedge against
	swings in the US stock market. And
	over the past 10 years, Winans Invest-
	ments Total Return, my all-US-stock
	model portfolio, has returned an annu-
	alized 9.3% versus 4.3% for the MSCI
	All Country World Index (ACWI), a
	benchmark that consists of roughly
	half US stocks and half foreign
HEARD BY OUR EDITORS	
Apathy may be an early sign of
	Alzheimer’s, says Zahinoor Ismail,
	MD, FRCPC. Families should watch
	older loved ones for emotional apathy
	and other behavioral changes such as
	suspicion or paranoia…greater-than-
	usual agitation or aggression…and
	loss of socially appropriate behavior.
	If new and sustained, these behaviors
	may be signs of mild behavioral impair-
	ment, an early indicator of Alzheimer’s
	disease and other dementias.
	Zahinoor Ismail, MD, FRCPC, is a neuropsychia-
	trist and assistant professor of psychiatry and
	neurology at Hotchkiss Brain Institute, University
	of Calgary, Canada.	
Investment opportunity: Urban re-
	newal bonds, we hear from Marilyn
	Cohen. These bonds are used for tax	
increment financing (TIF) for redevel-
	opment projects. Because the projects
	are not always successful, the feder-
	ally tax-exempt yields are higher than
	is typical. Investors
	should select proj-
	ects where most or
	all of the redevelop-
	ment is completed.	
Example: District of
	Columbia Tax Incre-
	ment Revenue Refunding Bonds, rated
	AA, which yield 1.2% annually until
	2021, then 3% to maturity in 2027. Ask
	your broker about TIF bonds, or search
	at Emma.MSRB.org.
	Marilyn Cohen is CEO of Envision Capital Man-
	agement, a fixed-income money manager, El
	Segundo, California. EnvisionCap.com	
Valley fever often is misdiagnosed,
	with deadly consequences, warns
	John Galgiani, MD. The disease,
	caused by fungi that live in desert
	soil, is clinically indistinguishable from
	many other diseases with severe re-
	spiratory symptoms. If you live in
	or have visited the West or South-
	west—especially Arizona or central
	California—and have pneumonia or
	flu symptoms, ask for a blood test for
	valley fever.
John Galgiani, MD, is professor of medicine at
	University of Arizona College of Medicine and
	director of the university’s Valley Fever Center
	for Excellence, both in Tucson.
> >

VOLUME 37
NUMBER 18
SEPTEMBER 15, 2016 / $5
36YEARS / INSIDE INFORMATION FROM THE WORLD’S BEST EXPERTS
The All-US Stock Portfolio
	Why This Adviser Shuns Foreign Stocks	
Kenneth G. Winans
Best
natural cures

8 SUMMER 2017
Because they grow so rapidly, cancer
cells crave sugar.
They get it from your blood. Which
is why, to prevent or eliminate cancer, the
last thing you want is high blood sugar.
The solution can be as simple as a
common vitamin.
Because when you get enough of this
vitamin, you cut the amount of blood
sugar that enters your cells by half.
No wonder dozens of studies show
this vitamin can protect against cancer.
In fact, it works so well that many
alternative doctors are now using it in a
special form as a cancer treatment.
You’ll get all the details on page 100 of
Doctors’ Best New Medical and Natural
Healing Breakthroughs.
But don’t stop there. Your free book is
packed with cancer fighters:
n The diet that reverses early-stage prostate
cancer—which many men have but don’t know
it. Permanently lowers PSA levels. Page 177
n How calcium prevents breast cancer.
What every woman must know. Page 160
n The plant extract that orders cancer cells
to commit suicide. Proven effective in over
100 studies! See page 99.
n Bounce back quicker from chemo. What
to eat the week before for more energy
and less fatigue! Page 161
n The newly discovered connection
between aspirin and colon cancer.
Page 149
n How a psychologist beat terminal cancer
—after his doctors gave up on him. Page 156
n Deactivate cancer-causing toxins in
your body. Just eat the foods on page 99
every day.
n Better than mammography. For certain
women, this new technique finds more
and smaller tumors. See page 102 to find
out if you’re one of them.
n The pain reliever that stops deadly colon
cancer by disrupting its growth. Page 104
Delicious drink
deactivates arthritis
by shutting down
its gene
Wouldn’t it be great to shut down
arthritis inflammation by
shutting it down genetically?
Now you can.
Scientists have recently discovered
that a popular drink contains a special
chemical compound that actually sup-
presses a key gene involved in arthritis
inflammation.
See page 121 of Doctors’ Best
New Medical and Natural Healing
Breakthroughs for this delicious prescrip-
tion for arthritis relief.
Then read on for lots more pain-
relieving advice:
n Relieve painful joints without drugs,
surgery, injections or herbs. Simple
do-it-yourself treatment used by many
athletes who cannot take pills or submit
to surgery. Page 121
n The 3 biggest back pain lies doctors
still spread. And a top back doctor’s
simple 3-step relief plan that really works.

cancer by cutting off its food supply
STEP 4: ENJOY YOUR MONEY... GET AND STAY HEALTHY
No matter how much money you have, you can’t enjoy it when you don’t feel your best. And you can’t hold on to your money when you have
enormous healthcare bills to pay. You can’t depend on doctors, hospitals or drug companies to give you the unbiased advice you need.
But once again, you can depend on our experts…
The Common Vitamin that Starves Cancer
by Cutting Off its Food Supply
Title photo: iStock.com/
Bottom Line Personal interviewed Todd
	Rosenbluth, senior director overseeing mutual
	funds for S&P Global Market
	Intelligence, which provides in-
	vestment research and analytical
	tools to more than 4,000 invest-
	ment banks, private-equity firms
	and financial-services clients,
	New York City. SPCapitalIQ.com
have flavo
No matter how buoyant the
	stock market has been through
	much of this year, many inves-
	tors have continued to worry
	about what could go wrong. Their fears
	include the possibility of an ill-timed
	increase in interest rates by the Federal
	Reserve…wrongheaded policies by a
	new US president…turmoil in Europe
	as the UK prepares to leave the Euro-
	pean Union…and the fear that stock
	prices have simply outpaced corporate
	profits.
If you share any of these concerns
	or have another reason to question this
	year’s high stock prices, you may want to
	“play defense” with at least a portion of
	your portfolio. Mutual fund expert Todd
	Rosenbluth says the best way to do that
	without abandoning stocks altogether is
	to use funds whose managers are masters
	of defense. That means the funds are
	likely to hold up better than the overall
	market in downturns but continue to
	provide healthy gains if the market does
well. Here’s how Rosenbluth identifies
	such funds and how you can use them
	in your portfolio…
3 KEY CHARACTERISTICS	
Playing defense has become tricky at
	this point in the seven-year-old bull
	market. Traditional strategies that inves-
	tors have relied on in the past to protect
	themselves during downturns may not
	be effective.
Example: Stocks of companies with
	long histories of reliable earnings and
	paying dividends typically are attractive
	as defensive plays. But many of these
	stocks have become quite overvalued,
	bid up by income-seeking investors.
	Another traditionally popular
HEARD BY OUR EDITORS	
Cheaper alternatives to EpiPens,	
we hear from Jay M. Portnoy, MD.
	Although the list price of EpiPen,
	Mylan’s emergency allergy treatment,
	has gone up astronomically, dis-
	counts are widely available through
	doctors and the company itself…
	and insurance generally covers most
	of the expense. Mylan also has been
	working on a less expensive generic
	version of the EpiPen. Also: A product
	called Adrenaclick contains the same
	dosage as EpiPen and often costs
	less. Caution: A syringe you preload
	yourself is not a good option because
	oxygen degrades epinephrine.
	Jay M. Portnoy, MD, is director of the division
	of allergy, asthma and immunology at Children’s
	Mercy Hospital, Kansas City, Missouri.	
You may get a break from the IRS
	if you miss a rollover deadline, says
	Ed Slott, CPA. If a transfer of assets
	from a 401(k) or other retirement ac-
	count into an IRA is not completed
	within 60 days, the assets may be tax-
	able and subject to penalties. Under a
	new IRS rule, you can get a waiver if
	you “self-certify” that one or more of 11
	mitigating circumstances caused the
	delay. These include a death or serious
	illness in your family or severe damage
	to a primary residence. Search online
	for “IRS Revenue Procedure 2016–47.”
	Ed Slott, CPA, is president of Ed Slott and Com-
	pany, an IRA advisory firm, Rockville Centre, New
	York, and author of The Retirement Savings Time
	Bomb…and How to Defuse It. IRAHelp.com	
A diet to reduce blood pressure
	also relieves gout, reports Stephen
	P. Juraschek, MD, PhD. The DASH
	diet emphasizes whole grains, fruits,
	vegetables and low-
	fat dairy products
	and reduced intake
	of salt, red meats,
	sweets and saturat-
	ed fats. It significant-
	ly reduces uric acid, associated with
	gout. In some patients, the DASH diet
	works almost as well as medication. For
	more information, go to DASHDiet.org.
	Stephen P. Juraschek, MD, PhD, is a research
	fellow in general internal medicine at Johns Hop-
	kins University School of Medicine, Baltimore.
> >

Title photo: iStock.com/
Bottom Line Personal interviewed Johanna
	Youner, DPM, podiatrist and podiatric surgeon.
	She is founder of Healthy Feet NY, a private prac-
	tice in New York City. She also
	is a certified laser specialist for
	tattoo removal and a member of
	The American Society for Laser
	Medicine and Surgery. Healthy
	FeetNY.net and ParkAvenue
	LaserTreatment.com
have fla
Most of us—75% of Ameri-
	cans—will have foot prob-
	lems at least sometime in
	our lives. Think about the
	stresses that your feet endure. They’re
	subjected to significant impact pres-
	sure just from walking—and a lot more
	from running. They’re squeezed into
	tight shoes and stuffed into hot socks.
	But you can treat many foot prob-
	lems at home—without potent drugs
	or high-priced medical care.
	Natural remedies that work…	
arNICa For INJUrIes	
This homeopathic remedy has become
	the go-to treatment for athletes—
	including members of the US Men’s
	National Soccer Team—who need to
	reduce post-injury swelling, inflamma-
	tion and bruises.
Arnica pellets (taken internally),
	ointments, creams and gels contain	
thymol derivatives, compounds that
reduce inflammation. A study pub-
	lished in a rheumatology journal found
	that homeopathic arnica relieved pain
	as well as ibuprofen—without the side
	effects that often occur with traditional
	over-the-counter painkillers.	
How to use it: Quickly apply arnica
	gel, ointment or cream (or put five
	sublingual arnica pellets under your
	tongue) after you’ve banged or twisted
	your foot or ankle. It works best when
	it’s applied or taken within 10 minutes
	after an injury. Repeat the treatment
	three times a day until the pain is gone.
	For homeopathic products, I recom-
	mend Boiron, a leading manufacturer
	of these remedies. They’re available at
	pharmacies and supermarkets.
HEARD BY OUR EDITORS	
Investment opportunity: small-
	cap dividend-paying stocks, we
	hear from Jay Kaplan, CFA. Small-
	cap stocks generally are trading at
	prices that make them bargains in
	comparison with large-cap dividend
	payers. His favorites now: Specialty-
	footwear retailer DSW (DSW), recent
	yield 3.4%…auto-parts manufacturer
	Gentex (GNTX), 1.9%…investment
	bank Moelis & Co. (MC), 4.5%.
	Jay Kaplan, CFA, is portfolio manager of the
	Royce Small-Cap Value Fund (RVVHX), Royce
	Total Return Fund (RYTRX) and Royce Dividend
	Value Fund (RDVIX). RoyceFunds.com	
High doses of fish oil benefit heart
	attack patients, reports Raymond
	Y. Kwong, MD, MPH. In a six-month
	study, patients who suffered heart
	attacks and were given four grams per
	day of the omega-3 fatty acids found in
	fish oil showed a 5.4% improvement in
	heart function compared with a decline
	of 1.2% for patients given a placebo.
	The omega-3 fatty acids were given
	in addition to all standard post–heart
	attack treatments.
	Raymond Y. Kwong, MD, MPH, is director of car-
	diac magnetic resonance imaging at Brigham and
	Women’s Hospital, and associate professor of med-
	icine at Harvard Medical School, both in Boston.	
Get rewards for paying tuition with
	a credit card, says analyst Matt Schulz.
	Of the 300 largest
	US colleges, 255
	accept credit card
	payments for at least
	some types of tuition,
	including 110 that don’t charge a
	fee for using a card. Families using
	a card charged an average of $4,443
	for tuition in 2015–16, which could result
	in a reward of $88.86 on a 2% cash-
	back card. However, at colleges that
	charge a fee, which is typically 2.75%,
	it doesn’t make sense to use a card,
	nor does it make sense if you can’t pay
	the credit card balance immediately.
	For a list of colleges that accept credit
	cards, go to CreditCards.com/credit-
	card-news/colleges-charge-card-fee-
	survey-data.php.
Matt Schulz is senior industry analyst at
	CreditCards.com, which tracks industry trends.

BLP_10_01_16_P_01_08_CLx.indd 1 	9/2/16 9:32 AM	
Title photo: iStock.com/
To Live
	Longer,
	Cook This
	Way
Bottom Line Personal interviewed Helen Vlassara,
	MD, professor emeritus and former director
	of the Diabetes and Aging Division at Icahn
	School of Medicine at Mount Sinai in New
	York City. She is an endocrinologist and a
	pioneer in the study of advanced glycation
	end products and their effects on health and
	aging. She is coauthor, with Sandra Woodruff,
	MS, RD, and Gary E. Striker,
	MD, of Dr. Vlassara’s AGE-Less
	Diet: How Chemicals in the
	Foods We Eat Promote Disease,
	Obesity and Aging, and the
	Steps We Can Take to Stop It.
	TheAge-LessWay.com
have flavo
When it comes to our health,
	we often focus on what to
	eat and what not to eat. But
	just as important is how
	we prepare the foods we eat. Certain
	cooking methods can unleash chemical
	by-products that have been linked to
	heart disease, diabetes, Alzheimer’s and
	other chronic diseases.
GLYCOTOXINS	
Sugar is a clingy molecule that attaches
	to amino acids and fats and changes
	their structures—a process known
	as glycation. This triggers a complex
	chemical reaction that culminates in
	the production of advanced glycation
	end products (AGEs). They’re some-
	times called “glycotoxins” because they
	trigger inflammation and can lead to
cell injury and cell death.
	Almost all foods contain AGEs.
	They’re naturally produced by the body
	as well. But their number vastly increas-
	es during food preparation, particularly
	when you cook with dry, high heat.
	Small amounts of AGEs aren’t a prob-
	lem—most are excreted through the kid-
	neys. But the foods that many people
	prefer—particularly those that are high
	in sugar and fat and are cooked certain
	ways—are teeming with AGEs. The
	body can’t cope with the excess, so the
	AGEs pile up over time. This leads to…	
More heart disease. AGE-modified
	proteins and fats can accumulate in
	blood vessel walls and stimulate clots—
	the cause of most heart attacks.
HEARD BY OUR EDITORS	
Shrewd way to get helpful cus-
	tomer service when phoning fails,
	advises advocate Edgar Dworsky.
	Check online forums where a compa-
	ny’s employees and customers address
	issues such as how to change a ser-
	vice contract or get a charge removed.
	(To find a forum, look under “Help”
	on the company website or enter the
	company’s name into a search engine
	with the words “forum” and “custom-
	er.”) Some forums allow you to send
	a question to an employee. Example:	
A Comcast customer saved $2,000
	by scaling down a service plan at
	Comcast/Xfinity (Forums.Xfinity.com).
	Edgar Dworsky is founder of the consumer web-
	sites ConsumerWorld.org and MousePrint.org.	
New treatment improves hand
	movement after a stroke, reports
	Jayme Knutson, PhD. Therapists
	often use electrical stimulation to
	elicit contractions in weakened hand
	muscles. In the new therapy, the pa-
	tient uses a sensor-equipped glove on
	the unaffected hand. When he opens
	his “good” hand, the
	other one receives
	corresponding stim-
	ulation—strengthen-
	ing the muscles and
	possibly changing
	neural connections.
	Moderately impaired patients who used
	the glove showed more than twice the
	improvement of patients using stimula-
	tion on the weaker hand.
	Jayme Knutson, PhD, is senior staff scientist at
	MetroHealth Medical Center, Cleveland.	
Stock opportunity: Small asset-
	management firms have become
	takeover targets, says analyst Mac
	Sykes. Examples: UK-based Henderson
	Group is acquiring Janus Capital Group
	in a $2.6 billion deal, and TD Ameritrade
	is acquiring Scottrade Financial for
	$4 billion. Potential takeover targets:	
Cohen & Steers (CNS) specializes in
	real estate and commodities…Waddell
	& Reed (WDR) has a nationwide network
	of 1,800 financial advisers.
	Mac Sykes is an equity analyst with Gabelli &
	Company, Rye, New York. Gabelli.com
> >

VOLUME 37
NUMBER 23
D ECEMBER 1, 2016 / $5

Helen Vlassara, MD Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai	Helen Vlassara, MD Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai
Title photo: iStock.com/
have flavo
What if you have a spare
	$1,000? Perhaps the money
	is a tax refund…a lottery
	win…an inheritance…or
	some other windfall.
	How about not spending the money
	but investing it instead? Don’t let the
	fact that it’s “just” $1,000 stop you.
	There are a number of especially smart
	things to do with a thousand (or a few
	thousand) bucks whether you want to
	save up for a special purchase…add to
	your nest egg…or even invest the money
	as a gift for your kids or grandchildren.
	To help our readers choose what to do
	with their next thousand, Bottom Line
	Personal spoke with five investment
	experts. The right investment choice
	for you will depend, in part, on when
	you might need to draw on the money
	and what your risk tolerance is…	
GET 3.5% ON A SAVINGS BOND	
Surprisingly, you (or your gift recipi-
	ent) can double the $1,000 in 20 years
	with a US savings bond. That’s because
	Series EE bonds will pay a guaranteed
	3.5% annual return. The catch is that
	the money must stay in the savings
	bond for 20 years to be a good invest-
	ment—if it is redeemed early, it will
	pay only 0.1% annually. But that 3.5%
is much better than what you can get
	on a 20-year US Treasury Bond, which
	currently yields about 2.2%. You can
	buy and sell EE bonds through an on-
	line account at TreasuryDirect.gov. You
	generally can’t redeem in the first year,
	and if you redeem in the first five years,
	you forfeit three months’ interest.	
Brian J. Kurtz is an estate-
	planning specialist with Assured
	Investment Planners, Troy, Michi-
	gan, and author of Getting the Most
	Out of Your Savings Bonds.
GET 2% ON A CD	
Yields on CDs are so low right now
	that you need to choose one with a
HEARD BY OUR EDITORS	
Weight gained from Thanksgiving
	until around New Year’s takes five
	months to lose, warns Brian Wansink,
	PhD. During the holiday season: Except
	for salad and vegetables, keep all other
	serving dishes off the table when eating.
	Place them on a counter. That way you
	have to leave the table to get seconds—
	and so are less likely to do so. To limit
	the temptation of leftovers: Keep them
	out of sight by freezing them or wrap-
	ping them in aluminum foil.
	Brian Wansink, PhD, is director of Cornell Food
	and Brand Lab, Ithaca, New York, and author of	
Slim by Design and coauthor of a study of holiday
	weight gain, published in The New England Jour-
	nal of Medicine.
Out-of-network ATM fees have
	climbed to a record $4.57 per transac-
	tion, on average (that’s $237.64 a year
	if you use an ATM once a week), says
	personal finance expert Greg McBride,
	CFA. This includes two separate fees—
	one charged by the financial institu-
	tion that provides the ATM (averaging
	$2.90) plus a second one by the ATM
	user’s own financial
	institution (averag-
	ing $1.67). To avoid
	out-of-network ATM
	fees: Check whether
	your bank offers a
	free smartphone app
	that can direct you to the closest fee-
	free ATM. Or use your debit card to get
	cash when you make a purchase at a
	retailer that offers fee-free cash back.
	Greg McBride, CFA, is chief financial analyst at
	personal-finance website Bankrate.com, which
	does an annual survey of ATM fees.	
Vasectomy does not increase
	prostate cancer risk as commonly
	believed, reports Sheldon Marks, MD.
	A new, very thorough study by epide-
	miologists from the American Cancer
	Society found no link between vasec-
	tomy—an effective and inexpensive
	form of long-term birth control—and
	prostate cancer.
Sheldon Marks, MD, is a urologist and micro-
	surgical specialist in private practice in Tucson,
	Arizona. The study of vasectomy and prostate
	cancer reviewed data on almost 364,000 men
	and was published in Journal of Clinical Oncology.
> >

The Best Ways to Invest

The Best Ways to Invest
	$1,000 Now
Title photo: iStock.com/
Bottom Line Personal interviewed Susan David,
	PhD, psychologist on the faculty of Harvard
	Medical School, Boston, and cofounder and co-
	director of the Institute of Coaching at McLean
	Hospital in Belmont, Massachusetts. She is
	author of Emotional Agility: Get
	Unstuck, Embrace Change and
	Thrive in Work and Life. If you
	want to assess how effective you
	are with your moods and emo-
	tions, a free quiz is offered at
	SusanDavid.com/learn.
have flav
Many people embrace hap-
	piness but try to push
	aside sadness and anger.
	Our culture encourages
	this—books about how to be happy
	frequently find their way onto best-
	seller lists, and “the pursuit of happi-
	ness” is written into our Declaration
	of Independence. Sadness and anger
	are viewed as problems to overcome
	on the road to happiness.
	But these so-called “negative” emo-
	tions can be useful. Here are some of
	the powerful benefits of bad moods and
	how to use them to your advantage…	
n Bad moods make us more careful
	and less gullible. When people are hap-
	py, they tend to have an “everything will
	be all right” mentality—which can get
	them into trouble when everything is
	not all right. When people are unhappy,
	on the other hand, their minds are on a
higher state of alert, carefully analyzing
	what they see and hear. That’s a useful
	mind-set to have when digging through
	potentially important details or dealing
	with slick salespeople.	
What to do: If you soon will need
	to be detail-oriented or skeptical—for
	example, if you’re on your way to nego-
	tiate a major purchase—you can inten-
	tionally put yourself in a negative mood.
	Recall an unhappy but not devastating
	memory, such as a time when your career
	hit a rough patch. Or listen to a piece of
	music that always makes you feel mel-
	ancholy—Samuel Barber’s “Adagio for
	Strings” may be a good choice.	
n Bad moods make us more con-
	vincing. People who are in negative
HEARD BY OUR EDITORS	
Avoid CDs with returns linked to
	stock market performance, says
	Keith Amburgey, CFA, CFP. Sold through
	banks and brokerages, market-linked	
CDs do not pay fixed annual yields…
	they cap their market-based gains, typi-
	cally at 5% per month…and monthly
	losses usually are capped but at a high
	30%. Market-linked CDs charge a com-
	mission as high as 3% on your initial
	investment, although they guarantee
	that you will end up with at least as
	much money as you invested. A recent
	study found that since 2010, nearly two-
	thirds of market-linked CDs underper-
	formed traditional five-year CDs.
	Keith Amburgey, CFA, CFP, is CEO of Rutherford
	Asset Planning, Tampa. RutherfordAsset.com	
Prostate cancer treatments com-
	pared in a new study, reports Jenny
	Donovan, PhD. Prostate cancer pa-
	tients with malignancies that haven’t
	spread and who are monitored but
	don’t receive treatment have a nearly
	identical risk for death over the next
	decade as those who choose surgery
	and/or radiation. Surgery and/or radi-
	ation (with possible side effects such
	as incontinence and/or loss of sexual
	function) lowered the risk of the cancer
	spreading but did not result in lower
	risk for death over 10 years. Note: Men
	with advanced or aggressive prostate
	cancer may need prompt treatment.
	Jenny Donovan, PhD, professor of social medi-
	cine, University of Bristol, UK.	
Best way to keep track of paper
	US savings bonds—convert them to
	electronic form, we hear from financial
	planner Jonathan D. Pond. This makes
	it easier to check
	a bond’s value
	and to redeem
	it. To learn how
	to switch, go to
	TreasuryDirect.
	gov, put “convert paper bonds” in the
	search box, and look on the results
	page for “Convert Your Paper Savings
	Bonds Using SmartExchange.”
	Jonathan D. Pond is president of Jonathan
	D. Pond, LLC, an investment advisory firm in
	Newton, Massachusetts. JonathanPond.com
> >

2 Don’t call me Nana

Why Bad
	Moods Are
	Good	
Susan David, PhD	
Harvard Medical School
VOLUME 37
NUMBER 21
NOVEMBER 1, 2016 / $5
36YEARS / INSIDE INFORMATION FROM THE WORLD’S BEST EXPERTS	
Do-good fund
	beats market	
Page 3
Title photo: iStock.com/
Bottom Line Personal interviewed Kenneth
	G. Winans, president and founder of Winans
	Investments, an investment advisory firm in
	Novato, California, and a former
	investment adviser at Merrill
	Lynch. He is coauthor of three
	books on investing, including	
Investment Atlas. He also writes
	a column for Forbes. Winans
	Investments.com
have flavo
The tumultuous world is a scary
	place for investors nowadays.
	Yet many advisers still tell
	American investors to keep
	their portfolios diversified by holding
	foreign stocks. The idea is that hold-
	ing a mixture of US and foreign stocks
	will reduce volatility and maybe even
	increase returns.
But is that really true? Is it good
	advice?
Kenneth G. Winans says that for
	Americans to think they have to hold
	foreign stocks is a big mistake. In fact,
	the veteran investment manager tells
	his clients that they don’t need to look
	outside the US at all. He says that’s
	especially true right now, when Europe
	is in an economic rut…China is bat-
	tling a sharp slowdown in growth…
	Japan has been unable to kick-start its
	stagnant economy…and low oil prices
	have pummeled countries ranging from
	Venezuela and Canada to Saudi Arabia
and Russia. And Winans believes the
	all-American approach makes sense for
	the long term, too.
	We asked him why he holds that be-
	lief and why investors should consider
	adopting this approach…	
GLOBAL DANGERS	
Foreign stocks have long been subpar
	performers and a poor hedge against
	swings in the US stock market. And
	over the past 10 years, Winans Invest-
	ments Total Return, my all-US-stock
	model portfolio, has returned an annu-
	alized 9.3% versus 4.3% for the MSCI
	All Country World Index (ACWI), a
	benchmark that consists of roughly
	half US stocks and half foreign
HEARD BY OUR EDITORS	
Apathy may be an early sign of
	Alzheimer’s, says Zahinoor Ismail,
	MD, FRCPC. Families should watch
	older loved ones for emotional apathy
	and other behavioral changes such as
	suspicion or paranoia…greater-than-
	usual agitation or aggression…and
	loss of socially appropriate behavior.
	If new and sustained, these behaviors
	may be signs of mild behavioral impair-
	ment, an early indicator of Alzheimer’s
	disease and other dementias.
	Zahinoor Ismail, MD, FRCPC, is a neuropsychia-
	trist and assistant professor of psychiatry and
	neurology at Hotchkiss Brain Institute, University
	of Calgary, Canada.
Investment opportunity: Urban re-
	newal bonds, we hear from Marilyn
	Cohen. These bonds are used for tax	
increment financing (TIF) for redevel-
	opment projects. Because the projects
	are not always successful, the feder-
	ally tax-exempt yields are higher than
	is typical. Investors
	should select proj-
	ects where most or
	all of the redevelop-
	ment is completed.	
Example: District of
	Columbia Tax Incre-
	ment Revenue Refunding Bonds, rated
	AA, which yield 1.2% annually until
	2021, then 3% to maturity in 2027. Ask
	your broker about TIF bonds, or search
	at Emma.MSRB.org.
	Marilyn Cohen is CEO of Envision Capital Man-
	agement, a fixed-income money manager, El
	Segundo, California. EnvisionCap.com	
Valley fever often is misdiagnosed,
	with deadly consequences, warns
	John Galgiani, MD. The disease,
	caused by fungi that live in desert
	soil, is clinically indistinguishable from
	many other diseases with severe re-
	spiratory symptoms. If you live in
	or have visited the West or South-
	west—especially Arizona or central
	California—and have pneumonia or
	flu symptoms, ask for a blood test for
	valley fever.
John Galgiani, MD, is professor of medicine at
	University of Arizona College of Medicine and
	director of the university’s Valley Fever Center
	for Excellence, both in Tucson.

SUMMER 2017 9
Two times of the year when risk of a heart attack soars
D o you know that there are
two times every year
when your heart attack
risk soars?
No, it’s not
Thanksgiving or
Christmas. Or your
birthday…
It’s when we reset the
clocks in the summer and fall.
Shocked researchers found
heart attack risk jumped as much as
10% on certain days right after we
summer ahead and fall behind.
How can this be?
It appears disrupting
sleep patterns can
both speed up your
heart and spark
inflammation and
artery damage. A
deadly combo!
To find out if
you’re at risk—and
exactly what to do if
you are—see page 154 of
Doctors’ Best New Medical and Natural
Healing Breakthroughs.
And to avoid heart problems or strokes
any time of year, check out:
n The only all-natural blood pressure fix that
also strengthens your heart. Page 144
n The grocery list statin makers don’t want
you to see. Because these foods lower your
cholesterol without their dangerous
drugs—for much less. Page 117
n Stop a stroke up to 8 hours after it starts.
New device removes clots even after it’s too
late for drugs. Page 131
n The only scientifically proven way to get rid
of deadly heart plaque. On page 247.
To avoid diabetes,
just do this outside
for 15 minutes
You may have heard about how
vitamin D can help you avoid
cancer and all sorts of health problems.
Now, one of America’s top natural phy-
sicians, Dr. Mark Stengler, tells us on page
43 that it can also prevent diabetes.
That’s because vitamin D both protects
the insulin-producing cells in the pancreas
and improves your glucose uptake.
What’s the best way to get your dose of
super-protective D? Ten to 15 minutes out-
side in the sun daily—without sunscreen.
And if you or someone you love has
diabetes, you’ll definitely want to see page
144 of Doctors’ Best New Medical and
Natural Healing Breakthroughs, where
you’ll discover…
n How 9 out of 10 diabetics were able to
discontinue their drugs—simply by eating
a lot less of one food. And it wasn’t sugar!
When movie stars need to look thinner,
younger and more glamorous, they
have a little trick that has nothing to do
with clothes or lighting.
In fact, it actually
makes them look
10 pounds thinner,
instantly.
You can use it
too, whether
you’re a movie
star or not.
Famed anti-aging expert David Ryback
reveals all on page 109 of Doctors’ Best
New Medical and Natural Healing
Breakthroughs.
Want to do more than look like you’ve
lost weight? Then don’t miss:
n The healthy snack that shuts off your
appetite—by making you feel full.
Page 146
n The drink that sends fat-burning into
overdrive. Page 132
n The spice that peels off the pounds.
Just add a pinch to lose an inch — or more.
On page 146.
Movie star trick makes you look 10 pounds thinner—
and years younger Bottom Line Personal
changed my life. I lost
30 pounds. I bought many
stocks that have tripled.
Thanks to you I made
money and feel better
and happier!
—Edward E. Polcha
Lindenhurst, NY

D id you know that the smell of one
particular type of pie is not only
sexually stimulating to men, it can
actually increase blood flow to the
penis…
…just as Viagra does?
And that a woman’s libido can get
a boost from the smell of a certain
flavoring used in candy.
You’ll find these and other passion-
pumping scents on page 126 of Doctors’
Best New Medical and Natural
Healing Breakthroughs.
We believe good health also means
good sexual health and enjoyment.
Which is why we think you should
also know…
n Doing this within 48 hours of a prostate
exam can lead to a false high PSA reading.
Page 177
n A natural way to slow down male
pattern baldness. Page 115
n Simple sex technique triples the odds
she’ll have an orgasm. Page 114
n Shut off the “hot flash hormone.”
Powerful herb blocks the chemical that
heats you up. Page 111
n All-natural passion inducers for men and
women. These “love drug” supplements
are safe enough to take daily. Page 112
n Don’t let a hospital fix your enlarged
prostate. These 2 new alternatives are
simple and safe enough to be done
in a doctor’s office. Page 137
The surprising
number one cause
of memory loss
Please don’t let anyone tell you that
memory loss is inevitable as you age.
Many things can cause memory
loss. And if you fix those causes,
memory often returns.
For example, on page 126 of Doctors’
Best New Medical and Natural Healing
Breakthroughs, you’ll find today’s
number one cause of memory loss.
And it’s very treatable.
If you’re concerned about improving
and preserving your memory, you’ll also
want to see:
n The doctor’s office cure for memory
loss. In some cases, it restores memory
in just 24 hours. Page 164
n The simple vitamin that can reverse
memory loss. Can it reverse yours?
See page 126!
n Banish Alzheimer’s in an hour.
You’re much less likely to develop it if
you do this for just one hour every
week. Page 132
Unnecessary tests and treat-
ments cost us billions of
dollars every year.
Plus, they cause
discomfort, pain and
other consequences
and complications.
David H. Newman,
MD, of Mount Sinai
School of Medicine, tells
you when to “just say no” to
the medical tests and treatments
on page 173 of Doctors’ Best New
Medical and Natural Healing Breakthroughs.
(Don’t miss the one question you should
always ask if offered an antibiotic.)
For your own health and safety—as well
as that of your loved ones—it pays to get the
facts from medical pros on the inside who
can tell you…
n The easy, surefire way to
get preferential treat-
ment in any hospital.
See page 246.

in any emergency
room. Plus, how to
choose the right ER.

n Symptoms that require
immediate medical
attention. Could easily save a
life. See page 171.
n The insider term that gets an ambulance to
your door faster in an emergency. Page 263
n What you should always bring to the
hospital. (Hint: It’s not something you would
wear or carry.) Page 246

Notorious con man reveals…
How to make yourself
identity-theft proof
Frank Abagnale’s early life as an
identity thief inspired the movie
Catch Me If You Can.
Today, he’s a world-renowned expert
on preventing identity and data theft,
and just one of the experts who, on
page 60 of Money and Retirement Secrets
of the Super Rich, will show you how to
build a brick wall around your data
and your identity.
Because you’re about to discover…
n Are you sure you’ve erased those files
from your computer? Most likely they’re
still on your hard drive. See page 216
to erase them permanently.
n How muggers, carjackers and
pickpockets rip you off. How to protect
yourself. See page 221.
n The simple 5-minute fix that instantly
shuts off unwanted emails! Page 224
n How to instantly tell if an ATM is rigged.
A billion dollars a year is lost to thieves
“skimming” off ATM withdrawals.
Don’t let any of it be yours. Page 216
n Identify a bogus email with this 2-
second test. Before it infects your
computer. Page 248
Why you should
put your cell phone
in the microwave
There’s a reason medical personnel
leave the room during X-rays:
Radiation exposure.
Perhaps you should be just as cautious
about your microwave. Because just about
all of them leak some dangerous radiation.
And here’s how to tell if yours leaks a
lot: Put your cell phone inside, close the
door, and dial the number. Make sure not
to turn on the microwave.
If it rings, that means the signal could
pass through into the oven.
That means dangerous
microwaves are get-
ting out. Be sure to
stay out of the
kitchen while
this is going on!
Technology
can be a double-
edged sword.
But knowing
facts like these
starting on page
157 of Doctors’ New
Medical and Natural
Healing Breakthroughs can keep
you safe. For example:
n Shocking! Electrical outlets are making
people sick. They can cause fatigue,
headaches, difficulty concentrat-
ing and cardiac symptoms.
Easy fix in your free book.
n Is your TV flooding your
living room with harmful
radiation? How to tell
and what to do about it.
n Cell phones can
increase your risk of
certain tumors by up to
100%. Here are 5 ways to pro-
tect yourself—without talking
any less.
STEP 5: SAFEGUARD YOUR WEALTH AND YOURSELF
Discover how technology is bringing new dangers—and new ways to protect yourself. Don’t let some scammer come along and drain your
bank account — or steal your identity.
Title photo: iStock.com/
Bottom Line Personal interviewed Todd
	Rosenbluth, senior director overseeing mutual
	funds for S&P Global Market
	Intelligence, which provides in-
	vestment research and analytical
	tools to more than 4,000 invest-
	ment banks, private-equity firms
	and financial-services clients,
	New York City. SPCapitalIQ.com
have flavo
No matter how buoyant the
	stock market has been through
	much of this year, many inves-
	tors have continued to worry
	about what could go wrong. Their fears
	include the possibility of an ill-timed
	increase in interest rates by the Federal
	Reserve…wrongheaded policies by a
	new US president…turmoil in Europe
	as the UK prepares to leave the Euro-
	pean Union…and the fear that stock
	prices have simply outpaced corporate
	profits.
If you share any of these concerns
	or have another reason to question this
	year’s high stock prices, you may want to
	“play defense” with at least a portion of
	your portfolio. Mutual fund expert Todd
	Rosenbluth says the best way to do that
	without abandoning stocks altogether is
	to use funds whose managers are masters
	of defense. That means the funds are
	likely to hold up better than the overall
	market in downturns but continue to
	provide healthy gains if the market does
well. Here’s how Rosenbluth identifies
	such funds and how you can use them
	in your portfolio…	
3 KEY CHARACTERISTICS	
Playing defense has become tricky at
	this point in the seven-year-old bull
	market. Traditional strategies that inves-
	tors have relied on in the past to protect
	themselves during downturns may not
	be effective.
Example: Stocks of companies with
	long histories of reliable earnings and
	paying dividends typically are attractive
	as defensive plays. But many of these
	stocks have become quite overvalued,
	bid up by income-seeking investors.
	Another traditionally popular
HEARD BY OUR EDITORS	
Cheaper alternatives to EpiPens,	
we hear from Jay M. Portnoy, MD.
	Although the list price of EpiPen,
	Mylan’s emergency allergy treatment,
	has gone up astronomically, dis-
	counts are widely available through
	doctors and the company itself…
	and insurance generally covers most
	of the expense. Mylan also has been
	working on a less expensive generic
	version of the EpiPen. Also: A product
	called Adrenaclick contains the same
	dosage as EpiPen and often costs
	less. Caution: A syringe you preload
	yourself is not a good option because
	oxygen degrades epinephrine.
	Jay M. Portnoy, MD, is director of the division
	of allergy, asthma and immunology at Children’s
	Mercy Hospital, Kansas City, Missouri.	
You may get a break from the IRS
	if you miss a rollover deadline, says
	Ed Slott, CPA. If a transfer of assets
	from a 401(k) or other retirement ac-
	count into an IRA is not completed
	within 60 days, the assets may be tax-
	able and subject to penalties. Under a
	new IRS rule, you can get a waiver if
	you “self-certify” that one or more of 11
	mitigating circumstances caused the
	delay. These include a death or serious
	illness in your family or severe damage
	to a primary residence. Search online
	for “IRS Revenue Procedure 2016–47.”
	Ed Slott, CPA, is president of Ed Slott and Com-
	pany, an IRA advisory firm, Rockville Centre, New
	York, and author of The Retirement Savings Time
	Bomb…and How to Defuse It. IRAHelp.com	
A diet to reduce blood pressure
	also relieves gout, reports Stephen
	P. Juraschek, MD, PhD. The DASH
	diet emphasizes whole grains, fruits,
	vegetables and low-
	fat dairy products
	and reduced intake
	of salt, red meats,
	sweets and saturat-
	ed fats. It significant-
	ly reduces uric acid, associated with
	gout. In some patients, the DASH diet
	works almost as well as medication. For
	more information, go to DASHDiet.org.
	Stephen P. Juraschek, MD, PhD, is a research
	fellow in general internal medicine at Johns Hop-
	kins University School of Medicine, Baltimore.

VOLUME 37
NUMBER 20
OCTOBER 15, 2016 / $5	
36YEARS / INSIDE INFORMATION FROM THE WORLD’S BEST EXPERTS
Surprising
	health danger	
Page 7
SIX FUNDS
	That Are
	Masters of
	Defense
Todd Rosenbluth	
S&P Global Market Intelligence
They Help Protect
	Your Portfolio
Title photo: iStock.com/
Bottom Line Personal interviewed Johanna
	Youner, DPM, podiatrist and podiatric surgeon.
	She is founder of Healthy Feet NY, a private prac-
	tice in New York City. She also
	is a certified laser specialist for
	tattoo removal and a member of
	The American Society for Laser
	Medicine and Surgery. Healthy
	FeetNY.net and ParkAvenue
	LaserTreatment.com
have fla
Most of us—75% of Ameri-
	cans—will have foot prob-
	lems at least sometime in
	our lives. Think about the
	stresses that your feet endure. They’re
	subjected to significant impact pres-
	sure just from walking—and a lot more
	from running. They’re squeezed into
	tight shoes and stuffed into hot socks.
	But you can treat many foot prob-
	lems at home—without potent drugs
	or high-priced medical care.
	Natural remedies that work…	
arNICa For INJUrIes	
This homeopathic remedy has become
	the go-to treatment for athletes—
	including members of the US Men’s
	National Soccer Team—who need to
	reduce post-injury swelling, inflamma-
	tion and bruises.
	Arnica pellets (taken internally),
	ointments, creams and gels contain	
thymol derivatives, compounds that
reduce inflammation. A study pub-
	lished in a rheumatology journal found
	that homeopathic arnica relieved pain
	as well as ibuprofen—without the side
	effects that often occur with traditional
	over-the-counter painkillers.	
How to use it: Quickly apply arnica
	gel, ointment or cream (or put five
	sublingual arnica pellets under your
	tongue) after you’ve banged or twisted
	your foot or ankle. It works best when
	it’s applied or taken within 10 minutes
	after an injury. Repeat the treatment
	three times a day until the pain is gone.
	For homeopathic products, I recom-
	mend Boiron, a leading manufacturer
	of these remedies. They’re available at
	pharmacies and supermarkets.
HEARD BY OUR EDITORS	
Investment opportunity: small-
	cap dividend-paying stocks, we
	hear from Jay Kaplan, CFA. Small-
	cap stocks generally are trading at
	prices that make them bargains in
	comparison with large-cap dividend
	payers. His favorites now: Specialty-
	footwear retailer DSW (DSW), recent
	yield 3.4%…auto-parts manufacturer
	Gentex (GNTX), 1.9%…investment
	bank Moelis & Co. (MC), 4.5%.
	Jay Kaplan, CFA, is portfolio manager of the
	Royce Small-Cap Value Fund (RVVHX), Royce
	Total Return Fund (RYTRX) and Royce Dividend
	Value Fund (RDVIX). RoyceFunds.com	
High doses of fish oil benefit heart
	attack patients, reports Raymond
	Y. Kwong, MD, MPH. In a six-month
	study, patients who suffered heart
	attacks and were given four grams per
	day of the omega-3 fatty acids found in
	fish oil showed a 5.4% improvement in
	heart function compared with a decline
	of 1.2% for patients given a placebo.
	The omega-3 fatty acids were given
	in addition to all standard post–heart
	attack treatments.
	Raymond Y. Kwong, MD, MPH, is director of car-
	diac magnetic resonance imaging at Brigham and
	Women’s Hospital, and associate professor of med-
	icine at Harvard Medical School, both in Boston.	
Get rewards for paying tuition with
	a credit card, says analyst Matt Schulz.
	Of the 300 largest
	US colleges, 255
	accept credit card
	payments for at least
	some types of tuition,
	including 110 that don’t charge a
	fee for using a card. Families using
	a card charged an average of $4,443
	for tuition in 2015–16, which could result
	in a reward of $88.86 on a 2% cash-
	back card. However, at colleges that
	charge a fee, which is typically 2.75%,
	it doesn’t make sense to use a card,
	nor does it make sense if you can’t pay
	the credit card balance immediately.
	For a list of colleges that accept credit
	cards, go to CreditCards.com/credit-
	card-news/colleges-charge-card-fee-
	survey-data.php.
	Matt Schulz is senior industry analyst at
	CreditCards.com, which tracks industry trends.

OCTOBER 1, 2016 / $5

6 stocks that
	go against the tide

Johanna Youner, DPM	
Healthy Feet NY
BLP_10_01_16_P_01_08_CLx.indd 1 
9/2/16 9:32 AM
Title photo: iStock.com/
To Live
	Longer,
	Cook This
	Way
Bottom Line Personal interviewed Helen Vlassara,
	MD, professor emeritus and former director
	of the Diabetes and Aging Division at Icahn
	School of Medicine at Mount Sinai in New
	York City. She is an endocrinologist and a
	pioneer in the study of advanced glycation
	end products and their effects on health and
	aging. She is coauthor, with Sandra Woodruff,
	MS, RD, and Gary E. Striker,
	MD, of Dr. Vlassara’s AGE-Less
	Diet: How Chemicals in the
	Foods We Eat Promote Disease,
	Obesity and Aging, and the
	Steps We Can Take to Stop It.
	TheAge-LessWay.com
have flavo
When it comes to our health,
	we often focus on what to
	eat and what not to eat. But
	just as important is how
	we prepare the foods we eat. Certain
	cooking methods can unleash chemical
	by-products that have been linked to
	heart disease, diabetes, Alzheimer’s and
	other chronic diseases.
GLYCOTOXINS	
Sugar is a clingy molecule that attaches
	to amino acids and fats and changes
	their structures—a process known
	as glycation. This triggers a complex
	chemical reaction that culminates in
	the production of advanced glycation
	end products (AGEs). They’re some-
	times called “glycotoxins” because they
	trigger inflammation and can lead to
cell injury and cell death.
	Almost all foods contain AGEs.
	They’re naturally produced by the body
	as well. But their number vastly increas-
	es during food preparation, particularly
	when you cook with dry, high heat.
	Small amounts of AGEs aren’t a prob-
	lem—most are excreted through the kid-
	neys. But the foods that many people
	prefer—particularly those that are high
	in sugar and fat and are cooked certain
	ways—are teeming with AGEs. The
	body can’t cope with the excess, so the
	AGEs pile up over time. This leads to…	
More heart disease. AGE-modified
	proteins and fats can accumulate in
	blood vessel walls and stimulate clots—
	the cause of most heart attacks.
HEARD BY OUR EDITORS	
Shrewd way to get helpful cus-
	tomer service when phoning fails,
	advises advocate Edgar Dworsky.
	Check online forums where a compa-
	ny’s employees and customers address
	issues such as how to change a ser-
	vice contract or get a charge removed.
	(To find a forum, look under “Help”
	on the company website or enter the
	company’s name into a search engine
	with the words “forum” and “custom-
	er.”) Some forums allow you to send
	a question to an employee. Example:	
A Comcast customer saved $2,000
	by scaling down a service plan at
	Comcast/Xfinity (Forums.Xfinity.com).
	Edgar Dworsky is founder of the consumer web-
	sites ConsumerWorld.org and MousePrint.org.	
New treatment improves hand
	movement after a stroke, reports
	Jayme Knutson, PhD. Therapists
	often use electrical stimulation to
	elicit contractions in weakened hand
	muscles. In the new therapy, the pa-
	tient uses a sensor-equipped glove on
	the unaffected hand. When he opens
	his “good” hand, the
	other one receives
	corresponding stim-
	ulation—strengthen-
	ing the muscles and
	possibly changing
	neural connections.
	Moderately impaired patients who used
	the glove showed more than twice the
	improvement of patients using stimula-
	tion on the weaker hand.
	Jayme Knutson, PhD, is senior staff scientist at
	MetroHealth Medical Center, Cleveland.	
Stock opportunity: Small asset-
	management firms have become
	takeover targets, says analyst Mac
	Sykes. Examples: UK-based Henderson
	Group is acquiring Janus Capital Group
	in a $2.6 billion deal, and TD Ameritrade
	is acquiring Scottrade Financial for
	$4 billion. Potential takeover targets:	
Cohen & Steers (CNS) specializes in
	real estate and commodities…Waddell
	& Reed (WDR) has a nationwide network
	of 1,800 financial advisers.
	Mac Sykes is an equity analyst with Gabelli &
	Company, Rye, New York. Gabelli.com
> >

	3 Profit from the big shift

Page 3
Helen Vlassara, MD Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai	Helen Vlassara, MD Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai
Title photo: iStock.com/
have flavo
What if you have a spare
	$1,000? Perhaps the money
	is a tax refund…a lottery
	win…an inheritance…or
	some other windfall.
	How about not spending the money
	but investing it instead? Don’t let the
	fact that it’s “just” $1,000 stop you.
	There are a number of especially smart
	things to do with a thousand (or a few
	thousand) bucks whether you want to
	save up for a special purchase…add to
	your nest egg…or even invest the money
	as a gift for your kids or grandchildren.
	To help our readers choose what to do
	with their next thousand, Bottom Line
	Personal spoke with five investment
	experts. The right investment choice
	for you will depend, in part, on when
	you might need to draw on the money
	and what your risk tolerance is…	
GET 3.5% ON A SAVINGS BOND	
Surprisingly, you (or your gift recipi-
	ent) can double the $1,000 in 20 years
	with a US savings bond. That’s because
	Series EE bonds will pay a guaranteed
	3.5% annual return. The catch is that
	the money must stay in the savings
	bond for 20 years to be a good invest-
	ment—if it is redeemed early, it will
	pay only 0.1% annually. But that 3.5%
is much better than what you can get
	on a 20-year US Treasury Bond, which
	currently yields about 2.2%. You can
	buy and sell EE bonds through an on-
	line account at TreasuryDirect.gov. You
	generally can’t redeem in the first year,
	and if you redeem in the first five years,
	you forfeit three months’ interest.	
Brian J. Kurtz is an estate-
	planning specialist with Assured
	Investment Planners, Troy, Michi-
	gan, and author of Getting the Most
	Out of Your Savings Bonds.
GET 2% ON A CD	
Yields on CDs are so low right now
	that you need to choose one with a
HEARD BY OUR EDITORS	
Weight gained from Thanksgiving
	until around New Year’s takes five
	months to lose, warns Brian Wansink,
	PhD. During the holiday season: Except
	for salad and vegetables, keep all other
	serving dishes off the table when eating.
	Place them on a counter. That way you
	have to leave the table to get seconds—
	and so are less likely to do so. To limit
	the temptation of leftovers: Keep them
	out of sight by freezing them or wrap-
	ping them in aluminum foil.
	Brian Wansink, PhD, is director of Cornell Food
	and Brand Lab, Ithaca, New York, and author of	
Slim by Design and coauthor of a study of holiday
	weight gain, published in The New England Jour-
	nal of Medicine.
Out-of-network ATM fees have
	climbed to a record $4.57 per transac-
	tion, on average (that’s $237.64 a year
	if you use an ATM once a week), says
	personal finance expert Greg McBride,
	CFA. This includes two separate fees—
	one charged by the financial institu-
	tion that provides the ATM (averaging
	$2.90) plus a second one by the ATM
	user’s own financial
	institution (averag-
	ing $1.67). To avoid
	out-of-network ATM
	fees: Check whether
	your bank offers a
	free smartphone app
	that can direct you to the closest fee-
	free ATM. Or use your debit card to get
	cash when you make a purchase at a
	retailer that offers fee-free cash back.
	Greg McBride, CFA, is chief financial analyst at
	personal-finance website Bankrate.com, which
	does an annual survey of ATM fees.	
Vasectomy does not increase
	prostate cancer risk as commonly
	believed, reports Sheldon Marks, MD.
	A new, very thorough study by epide-
	miologists from the American Cancer
	Society found no link between vasec-
	tomy—an effective and inexpensive
	form of long-term birth control—and
	prostate cancer.
	Sheldon Marks, MD, is a urologist and micro-
	surgical specialist in private practice in Tucson,
	Arizona. The study of vasectomy and prostate
	cancer reviewed data on almost 364,000 men
	and was published in Journal of Clinical Oncology.
> >

The Best Ways to Invest

The Best Ways to Invest
	$1,000 Now	
The Best Ways to Invest
	$1,000 Now
Title photo: iStock.com/
Bottom Line Personal interviewed Susan David,
	PhD, psychologist on the faculty of Harvard
	Medical School, Boston, and cofounder and co-
	director of the Institute of Coaching at McLean
	Hospital in Belmont, Massachusetts. She is
	author of Emotional Agility: Get
	Unstuck, Embrace Change and
	Thrive in Work and Life. If you
	want to assess how effective you
	are with your moods and emo-
	tions, a free quiz is offered at
	SusanDavid.com/learn.
have flav
Many people embrace hap-
	piness but try to push
	aside sadness and anger.
	Our culture encourages
	this—books about how to be happy
	frequently find their way onto best-
	seller lists, and “the pursuit of happi-
	ness” is written into our Declaration
	of Independence. Sadness and anger
	are viewed as problems to overcome
	on the road to happiness.
	But these so-called “negative” emo-
	tions can be useful. Here are some of
	the powerful benefits of bad moods and
	how to use them to your advantage…	
n Bad moods make us more careful
	and less gullible. When people are hap-
	py, they tend to have an “everything will
	be all right” mentality—which can get
	them into trouble when everything is
	not all right. When people are unhappy,
	on the other hand, their minds are on a
higher state of alert, carefully analyzing
	what they see and hear. That’s a useful
	mind-set to have when digging through
	potentially important details or dealing
	with slick salespeople.	
What to do: If you soon will need
	to be detail-oriented or skeptical—for
	example, if you’re on your way to nego-
	tiate a major purchase—you can inten-
	tionally put yourself in a negative mood.
	Recall an unhappy but not devastating
	memory, such as a time when your career
	hit a rough patch. Or listen to a piece of
	music that always makes you feel mel-
	ancholy—Samuel Barber’s “Adagio for
	Strings” may be a good choice.	
n Bad moods make us more con-
	vincing. People who are in negative
HEARD BY OUR EDITORS	
Avoid CDs with returns linked to
	stock market performance, says
	Keith Amburgey, CFA, CFP. Sold through
	banks and brokerages, market-linked	
CDs do not pay fixed annual yields…
	they cap their market-based gains, typi-
	cally at 5% per month…and monthly
	losses usually are capped but at a high
	30%. Market-linked CDs charge a com-
	mission as high as 3% on your initial
	investment, although they guarantee
	that you will end up with at least as
	much money as you invested. A recent
	study found that since 2010, nearly two-
	thirds of market-linked CDs underper-
	formed traditional five-year CDs.
	Keith Amburgey, CFA, CFP, is CEO of Rutherford
	Asset Planning, Tampa. RutherfordAsset.com	
Prostate cancer treatments com-
	pared in a new study, reports Jenny
	Donovan, PhD. Prostate cancer pa-
	tients with malignancies that haven’t
	spread and who are monitored but
	don’t receive treatment have a nearly
	identical risk for death over the next
	decade as those who choose surgery
	and/or radiation. Surgery and/or radi-
	ation (with possible side effects such
	as incontinence and/or loss of sexual
	function) lowered the risk of the cancer
	spreading but did not result in lower
	risk for death over 10 years. Note: Men
	with advanced or aggressive prostate
	cancer may need prompt treatment.
	Jenny Donovan, PhD, professor of social medi-
	cine, University of Bristol, UK.	
Best way to keep track of paper
	US savings bonds—convert them to
	electronic form, we hear from financial
	planner Jonathan D. Pond. This makes
	it easier to check
	a bond’s value
	and to redeem
	it. To learn how
	to switch, go to
	TreasuryDirect.
	gov, put “convert paper bonds” in the
	search box, and look on the results
	page for “Convert Your Paper Savings
	Bonds Using SmartExchange.”
	Jonathan D. Pond is president of Jonathan
	D. Pond, LLC, an investment advisory firm in
	Newton, Massachusetts. JonathanPond.com
> >

Why Bad
	Moods Are

Susan David, PhD

VOLUME 37
NUMBER 21
NOVEMBER 1, 2016 / $5	
36YEARS / INSIDE INFORMATION FROM THE WORLD’S BEST EXPERTS
Do-good fund
	beats market	
Page 3
Title photo: iStock.com/
Bottom Line Personal interviewed Kenneth
	G. Winans, president and founder of Winans
	Investments, an investment advisory firm in
	Novato, California, and a former
	investment adviser at Merrill
	Lynch. He is coauthor of three
	books on investing, including	
Investment Atlas. He also writes
	a column for Forbes. Winans
	Investments.com
have flavo
The tumultuous world is a scary
	place for investors nowadays.
	Yet many advisers still tell
	American investors to keep
	their portfolios diversified by holding
	foreign stocks. The idea is that hold-
	ing a mixture of US and foreign stocks
	will reduce volatility and maybe even
	increase returns.
	But is that really true? Is it good
	advice?
Kenneth G. Winans says that for
	Americans to think they have to hold
	foreign stocks is a big mistake. In fact,
	the veteran investment manager tells
	his clients that they don’t need to look
	outside the US at all. He says that’s
	especially true right now, when Europe
	is in an economic rut…China is bat-
	tling a sharp slowdown in growth…
	Japan has been unable to kick-start its
	stagnant economy…and low oil prices
	have pummeled countries ranging from
	Venezuela and Canada to Saudi Arabia
and Russia. And Winans believes the
	all-American approach makes sense for
	the long term, too.
	We asked him why he holds that be-
	lief and why investors should consider
	adopting this approach…	
GLOBAL DANGERS	
Foreign stocks have long been subpar
	performers and a poor hedge against
	swings in the US stock market. And
	over the past 10 years, Winans Invest-
	ments Total Return, my all-US-stock
	model portfolio, has returned an annu-
	alized 9.3% versus 4.3% for the MSCI
	All Country World Index (ACWI), a
	benchmark that consists of roughly
	half US stocks and half foreign
HEARD BY OUR EDITORS	
Apathy may be an early sign of
	Alzheimer’s, says Zahinoor Ismail,
	MD, FRCPC. Families should watch
	older loved ones for emotional apathy
	and other behavioral changes such as
	suspicion or paranoia…greater-than-
	usual agitation or aggression…and
	loss of socially appropriate behavior.
	If new and sustained, these behaviors
	may be signs of mild behavioral impair-
	ment, an early indicator of Alzheimer’s
	disease and other dementias.
	Zahinoor Ismail, MD, FRCPC, is a neuropsychia-
	trist and assistant professor of psychiatry and
	neurology at Hotchkiss Brain Institute, University
	of Calgary, Canada.	
Investment opportunity: Urban re-
	newal bonds, we hear from Marilyn
	Cohen. These bonds are used for tax	
increment financing (TIF) for redevel-
	opment projects. Because the projects
	are not always successful, the feder-
	ally tax-exempt yields are higher than
	is typical. Investors
	should select proj-
	ects where most or
	all of the redevelop-
	ment is completed.	
Example: District of
	Columbia Tax Incre-
	ment Revenue Refunding Bonds, rated
	AA, which yield 1.2% annually until
	2021, then 3% to maturity in 2027. Ask
	your broker about TIF bonds, or search
	at Emma.MSRB.org.
	Marilyn Cohen is CEO of Envision Capital Man-
	agement, a fixed-income money manager, El
	Segundo, California. EnvisionCap.com	
Valley fever often is misdiagnosed,
	with deadly consequences, warns
	John Galgiani, MD. The disease,
	caused by fungi that live in desert
	soil, is clinically indistinguishable from
	many other diseases with severe re-
	spiratory symptoms. If you live in
	or have visited the West or South-
	west—especially Arizona or central
	California—and have pneumonia or
	flu symptoms, ask for a blood test for
	valley fever.
John Galgiani, MD, is professor of medicine at
	University of Arizona College of Medicine and
	director of the university’s Valley Fever Center
	for Excellence, both in Tucson.
> >

14 SUMMER 2017
Y ou’ve read this far. So it’s obvious you believe you could benefit from your
collection of FREE Special Reports, and your 6 free up-to-the-minute issues
of Bottom Line Personal.
Why not take advantage of this free no-risk, send-no-money offer and start
enjoying the wealth, health and other benefits right now?
Because while there is no risk in returning the reply card facing page 15,
the way things are going these days, there IS a big risk if you don’t send it
back: lost opportunities, lost wealth, lost successes, lost health, lost happiness,
and a lifetime of struggling to keep up.
Instead, in your Special Report and twice a month in Bottom Line Personal,
you will be getting advice that can make the difference for you (or someone
you love) in your finances, health and overall quality of life.
No money to send. No risk. No obligation
I know that these days you have to be skeptical. So let me spell out my
promises to you:
n The 6 Special Reports and 6 issues are absolutely FREE. You need
never return them for any reason.
n No need to send any money.
n There are no hidden strings or obligations.
n If you LOVE the life-changing advice in Bottom Line Personal, please
join us after your test drive—at the lowest discounted rate we offer.
No obligation, of course. But fair warning: After 6 issues, many
people feel they cannot live without Bottom Line Personal, and
become loyal subscribers.
Finally, I’d like you to do something for me. And for yourself.
As soon as you get your How to Prosper During the Coming Middle-Class
Squeeze collection of 6 FREE Special Reports, look for advice about your most
pressing financial, health or other concern.
Then take at least one piece of advice and act on it immediately.
That’s the best way I know how to find out exactly the kind of life-changing
impact these reports—and Bottom Line Personal—can have on your life.
Remember: The sooner you act, the sooner you begin escaping the Great
Middle-Class Squeeze.
The sooner you begin building and protecting your wealth and your health.
The sooner you take control of your life and start enjoying the success and
happiness you deserve.
And the sooner you can help friends and loved ones do the same.
It costs you nothing to start picking the brains of our experts. But it may
cost you a great deal not to.
How a crazy idea has
helped millions lead
more successful lives
My father, Marty Edelston,
founder of Bottom Line Inc.,
always liked to get an
edge—to find the “hidden
advantage.”
But not just for himself. He delighted in
finding insider secrets and telling them to
others who were being taken advantage of
by “the system.”
So he came up with what many thought
was a pretty crazy idea.
He began inviting the world’s top
experts to private group luncheons in New
York City to share their best advice about
money, health, taxes, investing, relationships,
and how to live better and more successfully.
Today those little lunches have grown
into our worldwide network of hundreds of
insiders and experts.
Many of these experts are inacces sible
to mainstream media, yet they don’t hesi-
tate to share with us the little-known facts
and trade secrets on topics that impact the
lives of our readers.
Today, life is even more challenging
than when Dad started his lunches with
the experts.
That is why my siblings and I continue
Dad’s mission: To keep you from getting
squeezed out of the middle class and into
a life of struggle by giving you the insider
advice you need to be as healthy and
wealthy as you can be.
Please join me by returning this card for
your free How to Prosper During the Great
Middle-Class Squeeze collection of 6 FREE
Special Reports, and your 6 FREE issues of
Bottom Line Personal.
Sarah Hiner,
President, Bottom Line Inc
Please hurry because…
THE SQUEEZE
IS TIGHTENING

SUMMER 2017 15
Here at Bottom Line Inc., we’re awfully proud of
how we keep our readers a step or two
ahead. And I guess I don’t have to tell you
how important it is to not get caught by sur-
prise these days—when everything can
change so suddenly and quickly. Check out
our prescient track record for yourself.
n Crash of 2000 — In late 1999, we warned
of a severe stock market downturn. Over the
next year, the dot.com bubble burst and the
NASDAQ plunged by almost half.
n Crash of 2008 — We warned of coming trouble in
early 2008, just a few months before the next big drop.
n The Real Estate Crash — In 2005, we said the
bottom was about to fall out of the real estate
market... a few months before it did with
a crash.
n Natural Healing — Years before main-
stream media “discovered” natural
healing, we were telling readers how
to reverse disease without drugs
or surgery.
n Dangerous Drugs — We warned of poten-
tially deadly side effects from statins and other
common drugs years before the FDA got around
to issuing warnings.
How Bottom Line Personal Keeps a
Step Ahead of Everyone Else
We’ve got cabinets bursting with letters
from grateful readers.
They tell us about the money we’ve
made—or saved—them.
They tell us about diseases reversed,
drugs they no longer need, pain they no
longer suffer.
Some say we’ve changed their life. A
few say we saved it.
Here is just a small sample:

several thousand dollars.”

every year

Palm Coast, FL 32142-6607
In an historic shift with far-reaching implications, we
are increasingly dividing into a nation of “haves” and
“have nots.”
And that division is sure to be accelerated under the
new administration.
Millions of hard-working Americans are in a health
care crisis, or a bankruptcy away from hard times.
Yet the little-known fact is that many are prospering.
In fact, over the next decade, 10 million households
(3,000 a day!) will see their net worth pass the one
million dollar mark.
INSIDE: Our top experts reveal the wealth-growing,
health-transforming secrets that will enable you to
PROSPER during the Great Middle-Class Squeeze.
SPECIAL EDITION
How to prosper during the
Great Middle-Class Squeeze
